is an [interim Australian bioregion](/wiki/Interim_Biogeographic_Regionalisation_for_Australia "Interim Biogeographic Regionalisation for Australia") and is both a structural entity and a depositional area, now preserved on the east coast of [New South Wales](/wiki/New_South_Wales "New South Wales"), [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia") and with some of its eastern side now subsided beneath the [Tasman Sea](/wiki/Tasman_Sea "Tasman Sea"). The basin is named for the city of [Sydney](/wiki/Sydney "Sydney"), on which it is centred.

299–252 million years ago; The settling and evolution of swamp forest, which would shape tremendous [coal](/wiki/Coal "Coal") measures. To note, Australia's coal is younger than the [Carboniferous](/wiki/Carboniferous "Carboniferous") coal of the [Northern Hemisphere](/wiki/Northern_Hemisphere "Northern Hemisphere").

252–247 million years ago; dark, high-carbon [Narrabeen shales](/wiki/Narrabeen_shale "Narrabeen shale") can be viewed at [Long Reef](/wiki/Long_Reef_(New_South_Wales) "Long Reef (New South Wales)") and [Narrabeen](/wiki/Narrabeen "Narrabeen"). The [Australian continent](/wiki/Australia_(continent) "Australia (continent)") was part of the [Gondwana](/wiki/Gondwana "Gondwana") supercontinent and the Sydney basin was situated within a depositional basin. The [Ashfield Shale](/wiki/Ashfield_Shale "Ashfield Shale"), which overlies the Sydney sandstone, indicates an alteration in river flow direction and its depositional style. That was when southeast-streaming rivers deposited fine grained sands and muds within a river delta which settled on a shallow sea's shore.[[11]](#cite_note-SOP-11)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Sedimentary basin and region in New South Wales, Australia

Region in New South Wales, Australia

The **Sydney Basin** is an [interim Australian bioregion](/wiki/Interim_Biogeographic_Regionalisation_for_Australia "Interim Biogeographic Regionalisation for Australia") and is both a structural entity and a depositional area, now preserved on the east coast of [New South Wales](/wiki/New_South_Wales "New South Wales"), [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia") and with some of its eastern side now subsided beneath the [Tasman Sea](/wiki/Tasman_Sea "Tasman Sea"). The basin is named for the city of [Sydney](/wiki/Sydney "Sydney"), on which it is centred.

Around 5,000 metres (16,000 ft) thick, the Sydney Basin consists of [Permian](/wiki/Permian "Permian") and [Triassic](/wiki/Triassic "Triassic") [sedimentary](/wiki/Sedimentary "Sedimentary") rocks, which stretches from [Newcastle](/wiki/Newcastle,_New_South_Wales "Newcastle, New South Wales") in the north to [Batemans Bay](/wiki/Batemans_Bay,_New_South_Wales "Batemans Bay, New South Wales") in the south, and west to the [Great Dividing Range](/wiki/Great_Dividing_Range "Great Dividing Range"). The basin is also home to the major centres of [Newcastle](/wiki/Newcastle,_New_South_Wales "Newcastle, New South Wales"), [Gosford](/wiki/Gosford "Gosford"), and [Wollongong](/wiki/Wollongong,_New_South_Wales "Wollongong, New South Wales"), as well as the state capital of [Sydney](/wiki/Sydney "Sydney"), and contains economically significant reserves of [coal](/wiki/Coal "Coal").

Sydney's famous [harbour](/wiki/Port_Jackson "Port Jackson") and the sculptured cliffs of the [Blue Mountains](/wiki/Blue_Mountains_(New_South_Wales) "Blue Mountains (New South Wales)") are signature formations of relatively hard upper strata of [sandstone](/wiki/Sandstone "Sandstone"). The basin contains the [UNESCO World Heritage](/wiki/UNESCO_World_Heritage_Site "UNESCO World Heritage Site")-listed [Greater Blue Mountains Area](/wiki/Greater_Blue_Mountains_Area "Greater Blue Mountains Area").[[3]](#cite_note-3)

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sydney_Basin&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Geography")]

According to [NSW Primary Industries](/wiki/Department_of_Primary_Industries_(New_South_Wales) "Department of Primary Industries (New South Wales)"), the basin extends through approximately 350 kilometres (220 mi) of coastline from Newcastle in the north to Durras Lake (near [Batemans Bay](/wiki/Batemans_Bay,_New_South_Wales "Batemans Bay, New South Wales")) in the south. From Durras Lake the western boundary continues in a line through [Lithgow](/wiki/Lithgow,_New_South_Wales "Lithgow, New South Wales") to around Ulan (near [Mudgee](/wiki/Mudgee "Mudgee")). To the north the boundary extends 120 kilometres (75 mi) along the [Liverpool Range](/wiki/Liverpool_Range "Liverpool Range") to a point 80 kilometres (50 mi) north of [Muswellbrook](/wiki/Muswellbrook,_New_South_Wales "Muswellbrook, New South Wales"), and then runs 200 kilometres (120 mi) back to the coast at Newcastle. To the east the basin continues to the edge of the [continental shelf](/wiki/Continental_shelf "Continental shelf").[[4]](#cite_note-dpi_sbsd-4)

The total area of the basin is approximately 44,000 square kilometres (17,000 sq mi) onshore plus 5,000 square kilometres (1,900 sq mi) offshore.[[5]](#cite_note-dpi_sbgo-5) The centre of the basin is located around 30 kilometres (19 mi) west of the [Sydney central business district](/wiki/Sydney_central_business_district "Sydney central business district") at [Fairfield](/wiki/Fairfield,_New_South_Wales "Fairfield, New South Wales"), though only the youngest Triassic ([middle Triassic](/wiki/Middle_Triassic "Middle Triassic")) rocks are exposed in the Sydney area.[[6]](#cite_note-am_dotsb-6)

The [Australian Government](/wiki/Government_of_Australia "Government of Australia") [Department of Climate Change, Energy, the Environment and Water](/wiki/Department_of_Climate_Change,_Energy,_the_Environment_and_Water "Department of Climate Change, Energy, the Environment and Water") classifies the basin as an [interim Australian bioregion](/wiki/Interim_Biogeographic_Regionalisation_for_Australia "Interim Biogeographic Regionalisation for Australia") consisting of 3,629,597 hectares (8,968,930 acres).[[7]](#cite_note-7) Meanwhile, according to [Geoscience Australia](/wiki/Geoscience_Australia "Geoscience Australia") the basin covers 64,000 square kilometres (25,000 sq mi), of which 36,000 square kilometres (14,000 sq mi) is onshore and 28,000 square kilometres (11,000 sq mi) is offshore with water depths of up to 4,500 metres (14,800 ft).[[2]](#cite_note-GA-2) Another Australian Government agency classifies that the basin covers approximately 25,000 square kilometres (9,700 sq mi).[[1]](#cite_note-bioregionalassessment-1)

## History and formation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sydney_Basin&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History and formation")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/24/View_from_North_Head_Lookout_-_panoramio.jpg/330px-View_from_North_Head_Lookout_-_panoramio.jpg)](/wiki/File:View_from_North_Head_Lookout_-_panoramio.jpg)

The majority of Sydney Basin is raised sandstone plateau, with the exception being the [Hunter Valley](/wiki/Hunter_Valley "Hunter Valley") and the low-lying [Cumberland Plain](/wiki/Cumberland_Plain "Cumberland Plain").

See also: [Geology of New South Wales](/wiki/Geology_of_New_South_Wales "Geology of New South Wales")

Minor igneous activity took place in the basin during the [Early Jurassic](/wiki/Early_Jurassic "Early Jurassic") (i.e. 210 million years ago), Late [Mesozoic](/wiki/Mesozoic "Mesozoic") (i.e. 100–90 million years ago) and [Cenozoic](/wiki/Cenozoic "Cenozoic") eras (i.e. 65 million years ago). The Early Jurassic activity resulted in the formation of the [Prospect dolerite intrusion](/wiki/Prospect_dolerite_intrusion "Prospect dolerite intrusion") in [Prospect Hill](/wiki/Prospect_Hill_(New_South_Wales) "Prospect Hill (New South Wales)").[[8]](#cite_note-8) Although Jurassic sedimentation is not observed in the Sydney Basin, there are Jurassic volcanic [breccia](/wiki/Breccia "Breccia") pipes ([diatremes](/wiki/Diatreme "Diatreme")).[[9]](#cite_note-Johnson1989-9)

The Sydney Basin is part of a major basin system that extends over 1,500 kilometres (930 mi) from the [Bowen Basin](/wiki/Bowen_Basin "Bowen Basin") in [Queensland](/wiki/Queensland "Queensland") through to the [Gunnedah](/wiki/Gunnedah,_New_South_Wales "Gunnedah, New South Wales") Basin in NSW. Onshore, the basin contains 4,500 metres (14,800 ft) of Permo-Triassic clastic sediments, while the offshore basin contains 6,000 metres (20,000 ft) of sediments. The basin overlies the [Lachlan Fold Belt](/wiki/Lachlan_Fold_Belt "Lachlan Fold Belt") and Late Carboniferous volcanoclastic sediments. The basin formed during extension in the [Early Permian](/wiki/Early_Permian "Early Permian"), with [half-graben](/wiki/Graben#Half-graben "Graben") infilled with the Dalwood and Talaterang Groups. Foreland loading followed with the compression of the Currarong Orogen in the Early Permian.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

[Late Permian](/wiki/Late_Permian "Late Permian") uplift associated with the [New England](/wiki/New_England_(Australia) "New England (Australia)") foreland loading phase resulted in the formation of depocentres with the northeast Sydney Basin with best preserved marine fossils. These depocentres filled with pyroclastic and alluvial-paludual sediments of the Newcastle Coal Measures. In the Triassic, uplift of the offshore basin resulted in reworking of Permian sediments in fluvial environments. The basin underwent a final phase of deformation (thrusting) in the [Middle Triassic](/wiki/Middle_Triassic "Middle Triassic") where it was uplifted to become dry land, with an erosion occurring from this time to the present.

Extension and breakup in the Tasman Sea beginning in the Late Cretaceous resulted in the current structural boundaries of the basin's eastern margin. In the south and west the Basin finishes in cliff lines formed on sandstones and [conglomerates](/wiki/Conglomerate_(geology) "Conglomerate (geology)") of the primary Permian sediments, with waterfalls being widespread on all escarpments.[[2]](#cite_note-GA-2)

### Timeline

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sydney_Basin&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Timeline")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/1/12/Prospectdolerite.jpg/250px-Prospectdolerite.jpg)](/wiki/File:Prospectdolerite.jpg)

The Prospect dolerite intrusion in [Greater Western Sydney](/wiki/Greater_Western_Sydney "Greater Western Sydney")

* **Permian:** 299–252 million years ago; The settling and evolution of swamp forest, which would shape tremendous [coal](/wiki/Coal "Coal") measures. To note, Australia's coal is younger than the [Carboniferous](/wiki/Carboniferous "Carboniferous") coal of the [Northern Hemisphere](/wiki/Northern_Hemisphere "Northern Hemisphere").
* **[Early Triassic](/wiki/Early_Triassic "Early Triassic"):** 252–247 million years ago; dark, high-carbon [Narrabeen shales](/wiki/Narrabeen_shale "Narrabeen shale") can be viewed at [Long Reef](/wiki/Long_Reef_(New_South_Wales) "Long Reef (New South Wales)") and [Narrabeen](/wiki/Narrabeen "Narrabeen"). The [Australian continent](/wiki/Australia_(continent) "Australia (continent)") was part of the [Gondwana](/wiki/Gondwana "Gondwana") supercontinent and the Sydney basin was situated within a depositional basin. The [Ashfield Shale](/wiki/Ashfield_Shale "Ashfield Shale"), which overlies the Sydney sandstone, indicates an alteration in river flow direction and its depositional style. That was when southeast-streaming rivers deposited fine grained sands and muds within a river delta which settled on a shallow sea's shore.[[11]](#cite_note-SOP-11)
* **Mid Triassic:** 247–235 million years ago; A monolithic river with its beginnings to the south-west of [Broken Hill](/wiki/Broken_Hill "Broken Hill"), in what was [Antarctica](/wiki/Antarctica "Antarctica") at that time,[[12]](#cite_note-12) had its delta in what was the Sydney Basin. It is around five times bigger than the [Amazon River](/wiki/Amazon_River "Amazon River"). There is predominance of [silica sand](/wiki/Silica_sand "Silica sand") with minor lenses of [clay](/wiki/Clay "Clay"). [Plant fossils](/wiki/Plant_fossil "Plant fossil") are scarce, but some fish fossils are found in the clay lenses.
* **[Late Triassic](/wiki/Late_Triassic "Late Triassic"):** 235–201 million years ago; As the river slowed with the erosion of the mountain range, finer shales were laid out. This strata is rich in [seed fern](/wiki/Seed_fern "Seed fern") fossils.
* **End of Triassic:** 201 million years ago; Ascension and shifting at the Lapstone fault, with the Blue Mountains rising and the [western Sydney plain](/wiki/Cumberland_Plain "Cumberland Plain") descending to a flat land and [Sydney CBD](/wiki/Sydney_CBD "Sydney CBD") jousting upward.
* **Jurassic:** 201–145 million years ago; Erosion, with [Ashfield Shales](/wiki/Ashfield_Shale "Ashfield Shale") remaining on top. Deep V-shaped valleys in the [Hawkesbury sandstone](/wiki/Hawkesbury_sandstone "Hawkesbury sandstone"). Fracturing, volcanic intrusions form [Prospect Quarry](/wiki/Prospect_Hill_(New_South_Wales) "Prospect Hill (New South Wales)"), [Mount Tomah](/wiki/Mount_Tomah "Mount Tomah"), [Mount Wilson](/wiki/Mount_Wilson,_New_South_Wales "Mount Wilson, New South Wales") and [Hornsby](/wiki/Hornsby,_New_South_Wales "Hornsby, New South Wales") Quarry.[[13]](#cite_note-13)
* **[Cainozoic](/wiki/Cainozoic "Cainozoic") ([Tertiary](/wiki/Tertiary_period "Tertiary period") and [Quaternary](/wiki/Quaternary "Quaternary")):** 66 million years to present; Gondwana broke up around 40 to 60 million years ago. That is when the Australian continent started to form where it drifted and rifted, where Sydney's rocks were elevated, canted and then later eroded by the weather. Sydney's [sedimentary rocks](/wiki/Sedimentary_rock "Sedimentary rock") were shaped into a landscape that was defined by [bedrock](/wiki/Bedrock "Bedrock") valleys exposed into a raised plateau. Sydney's largest rivers, such as the [Hawkesbury](/wiki/Hawkesbury_River "Hawkesbury River"), [Parramatta](/wiki/Parramatta_River "Parramatta River"), [Georges](/wiki/Georges_River "Georges River") and [Hacking Rivers](/wiki/Hacking_River "Hacking River") eroded the region's deepest valleys. In this period, the Ashfield Shale got weathered to create a flatter landform with low, undulating topography and reasonably fertile soils, which heavily contrasted the plateaus, cliffs and gorges on the sandstone areas in the Sydney Region.[[11]](#cite_note-SOP-11) The [Botany Bay](/wiki/Botany_Bay "Botany Bay") Basin was also developed at that time, which is infilled with sand.
* **Late [Pleistocene](/wiki/Pleistocene "Pleistocene"):** 12 000 years ago; Submerging the Sydney river valleys with the post-glacial [sea level rise](/wiki/Sea_level_rise "Sea level rise") where estuaries and deep harbours were formed.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

## Hydrology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sydney_Basin&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Hydrology")]

The [hydrology](/wiki/Hydrology "Hydrology") of the basin comprises three main [drainage basins](/wiki/Drainage_basin "Drainage basin") as defined by the New South Wales Office of Water that lie entirely or mainly within the geography of the basin; namely the [Central Coast](/wiki/Central_Coast_(New_South_Wales) "Central Coast (New South Wales)") catchment, the [Hawkesbury](/wiki/Hawkesbury_River "Hawkesbury River")-[Nepean](/wiki/Nepean_River "Nepean River") catchment, and the [Sydney Metropolitan](/wiki/Sydney "Sydney") catchment.

In addition, some of the rivers of the [Hunter](/wiki/Hunter_River_(New_South_Wales) "Hunter River (New South Wales)")-Central Rivers catchment and the Southern Rivers catchment also lie mainly in the basin. In the Hunter-Central Rivers catchment, the [Hunter River](/wiki/Hunter_River_(New_South_Wales) "Hunter River (New South Wales)") sub-catchment forms the northern boundary of the basin. In the Southern Rivers catchment, the [Illawarra](/wiki/Illawarra "Illawarra") sub-catchment and the [Shoalhaven](/wiki/Shoalhaven_River "Shoalhaven River") sub-catchment forms the southern boundary.
